comments.author: @TruePlanetOffical
comments.text:
If you love wild American landscapes & scenic documentaries, don't miss:
SOUTH DAKOTA: Forgotten Wonderland at the Edge of America | 4K Travel Documentary 🔥
https://youtu.be/f-nedXYitwM

comments.author: @badger86U
comments.text: The smoke/fog explained at 5:32

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Glad you noticed that part! If you enjoy these deep-dive travel docs, please like, share, and subscribe for more!

comments.author: @Wise489
comments.text: You said it's full of old growth forest but thats not true. Appalachia was heavily logged during the great depression. That's where almost all of the road systems come from on all the mountain ranges. I believe there are only two small patches of old growth.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text:
You’re absolutely right — thanks for the correction. Appalachia took a heavy hit during the Depression logging boom, and true old-growth pockets are pretty scarce these days. Appreciate you keeping me honest!
If you dig these deep-dive docs, hit subscribe, drop a like, and share it with a fellow trail lover!

comments.author: @RickySmith-zi5cz
comments.text: Jesus Is Lord.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you for sharing your faith. Wishing you God's blessings and safe travels wherever life takes you. Thanks for being here with us! If you enjoy exploring America's hidden places, we'd love to have you subscribe, like, and share the journey.

comments.author: @HappyMonroe-o9i
comments.text:
I LIVE IN

WESTERN NORTH CAROLINA,
MCDOWELL COUNTY,
I GREW UP 39½ YEARS AGO IN OLD FORT, NORTH CAROLINA 2862
I'VE LIVED ALL OVER MCDOWELL COUNTY,
AT THE MOMENT I'M CURRENTLY LIVING IN WEST MARION, NORTH CAROLINA IN MY CAMPER CURRENTLY 40 YEAR'S OLD CURRENTLY SINGLE AND CURRENTLY LOOKING FOR A 420/MUSIC LOVER/TATTOOS...
AND NEEDING TO MOVE FROM MY LOCATION...

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks for sharing your story. Western North Carolina is such a beautiful part of Appalachia, and we sincerely hope things work out for you. Wishing you all the best on your next chapter. If you enjoy documentaries like this, please consider subscribing, liking, and sharing—it really helps us keep exploring places like home.

comments.author: @edwinmorrill4916
comments.text: The applacachia go thru Philly and NYC? And states I've never heard of?!?!

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Appalachia doesn't include Philadelphia or New York City. It's a cultural and mountain region that stretches through parts of 13 states, which is why some of the lesser-known states might seem surprising. Thanks for watching! If you enjoy exploring places like this, don't forget to like, subscribe, and share the channel!

comments.author: @tnfirebird94
comments.text: Lost me when you said appalaychia. It’s now irrelevant.

replies.author: @tnfirebird94
replies.text: @TruePlanetOfficalapp-uh-latch-uh.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: I get why the pronunciation of Appalachia stands out, especially if you’re from the region. Which pronunciation do you hear most often where you live?

comments.author: @charlesbloom7909
comments.text: It’s almost like the mountains were normal then a giant flood swept across them 😂😂😂😂

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: You’re right that water played a huge role in shaping the Appalachians. The mountains themselves formed through ancient tectonic collisions, then millions of years of rivers, rain, and erosion carved them into the landscape we see today. Have you ever visited this part of the country?

comments.author: @LarryPerna-w8e
comments.text: Great video! I enjoyed the whole experience. Thank you!

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you so much! I’m really glad you enjoyed the journey. Was there one moment or place in the video that stayed with you most?

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks for watching and leaving a comment! I’m not quite sure what you meant by “0,” though—could you explain a little more?

comments.author: @ianblaha1459
comments.text: Nor we are not here to learn about txhtonic plates pangea really youtube should ban you

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: I hear you. This channel is about exploring places and the stories behind them, and I appreciate you sharing how you feel. What kind of content would you rather see in videos like this?

comments.author: @ianblaha1459
comments.text: Would be good minus all the ai 😢 crap

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: I hear you. We use AI carefully to help tell the story, but the places, culture, and real-world feeling will always come first. What would you like to see more of in future videos?

comments.author: @Earthstein
comments.text: Powerful for an old man like myself, who once lived in those "hills" back in the 50's. The earth seemly unconnected to the north California mountains I live in now. I appreciate this presentation.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you so much for sharing that. Hearing from someone who actually lived in those hills in the ’50s makes this video feel even more meaningful. I can only imagine how different it must feel compared to Northern California—what do you remember most about life there back then?

comments.author: @ETBigfoot4960
comments.text: It's a shame how those who produce these videos insert scenes of places nowhere near Appalachia. It's a disappointment to see drone shots of some abandoned mine in an arid Arizona type place while watching this video

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: You’re absolutely right to call that out. Appalachian stories deserve visuals that truly belong to the region, not generic footage from somewhere else. We’ll be more careful with that in future videos—thanks for keeping us honest.

comments.author: @EpicAdventureWorld
comments.text: People talk about the Rockies and the Grand Canyon all the time, but Appalachia has a different kind of magic. Older, quieter, and honestly deeper.

replies.author: @Harperartist
replies.text: God's Country.  The North Carolina mountains are the most beautiful in the world.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Absolutely — Appalachia has this quiet, lived-in beauty that really stays with you. It feels less like a place you just visit and more like a place you slowly get to know. Have you spent much time there yourself?

comments.author: @sandrasmith7091
comments.text: Salamanders imprisoned in wet soil¿ that was a weird term🤔look pretty satisfied to me.

replies.author: @sandrasmith7091
replies.text: ​@TruePlanetOfficaloh yes .use to catch them n crawdads at my papaws in east tenn as a kid. Where i live now there's Cave Mt lake. Part of natl park. A stream run i, on the mt. Guaranteed salamanders. We always release them after. Grandkids love it. Great little lake for young kids. Google it. Very sweet quiet place. Family friendly,  restrooms...

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Haha, yeah "imprisoned in wet soil" is a weird way to put it 😂 But you're right — they honestly look pretty happy and cozy down there. Have you ever seen salamanders in real life?

comments.author: @sandrasmith7091
comments.text: Thank you I love these facts. Dad from the Smokies mom wv. I'm va Shenandoah valley. In the middle of both places. Appalachia is home❤

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That's a beautiful connection to Appalachia. ❤️ You really do have roots in some special places. Is there one spot in the Smokies, West Virginia, or the Shenandoah Valley that always feels like home to you? I'd love to hear your favorite memory.

comments.author: @WanderWorld-x6m
comments.text: thank you!

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks! I really appreciate you being here. 😊 Was there one part of the video that stood out to you the most?

comments.author: @Earthcue
comments.text: The Appalachian Mountains are so ancient that they formed before the first animals ever walked on land — 480 million years ago! 🏔😱 Scientists believe they were once as tall as the Himalayas (think Everest-level heights!) but millions of years of erosion slowly wore them down.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That’s incredible to think about. Knowing these mountains were once Himalayan-sized makes the whole region feel even more powerful. Have you ever visited a part of Appalachia that really made you feel that history?

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: The culture and history of Appalachia are deeply moving. I love how the bluegrass music and unique dialects preserve a piece of history that time almost forgot.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: @wrtrwoman55 Hey, thanks so much for watching and for the kind words — really means a lot coming from a local! Appreciate you pointing out the correct pronunciation of “Appalachia.” I’ll make sure to get that right next time. Local details like that are exactly what make these places special.

replies.author: @wrtrwoman55
replies.text: @TruePlanetOffical This video is wonderful, as a resident of the Appalachian area, thank you for doing it. However, part of the charm of local speech is the pronunciation of 'Appalachia.' The 'a' in the middle is not the long 'a' sound, but the short 'a' sound, as in the word 'match'. I don't mean to quibble, but this the the way locals say it.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you so much — that’s exactly what makes Appalachia so special. The music, the voices, and the stories all carry so much history. Is there a particular bluegrass artist or Appalachian tradition you love most?

comments.author: @ericnasser4261
comments.text: Yeah like Bigfoot

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Haha, that's a fun comparison! 😄 Do you think Bigfoot is just a legend, or is there a small chance something unknown is still out there?

comments.author: @NomadicCorner
comments.text: This region deserves much more attention

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: I couldn't agree more. There are so many incredible places here that rarely get the spotlight they deserve. What was the one place that surprised you the most?

comments.author: @lorraineblass9875
comments.text: It does NOT stop in New York. It continues on to Mount Katdadin

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: You're absolutely right! It doesn't end in New York—reaching Mount Katahdin is an incredible finish. Have you hiked any part of the trail yourself, or is it still on your bucket list?

comments.author: @barbaramcarthur142
comments.text: That was good. Thank you.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you so much! I'm really glad you enjoyed it. Your support and kind words mean a lot and help keep me motivated to create more videos. Hope you'll enjoy the next one too!

comments.author: @TRUEGLOBAL-p7b
comments.text: The cultural traditions in this video are truly fascinating.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you! I'm really glad you enjoyed that part of the video. Cultural traditions are one of my favorite aspects to explore because they help tell the story of a place beyond its landscapes and landmarks.

comments.author: @mysteryvault-h2o
comments.text: I had no idea Appalachia was home to the highest concentration of salamanders on Earth or fireflies that synchronize their flashes. The landscapes are beautiful, but it's these little details that make the region feel almost magical. Has anyone here hiked part of the Appalachian Trail?

replies.author: @tclamb
replies.text: I love everything about nature, and all the living creatures who let us share the planet with them. These salamanders are beautiful in their uniqueness. 💖

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thank you for sharing such a detailed perspective. It means a lot to hear from someone who calls the Smokies home. Thanks again—and if you think others would enjoy this story, feel free to share the video.

replies.author: @Rob5215life
replies.text:
​@TruePlanetOfficali would say winter is the only season thats a little tough . 
We have 4 true seasons here.   The temps slowly increase to about 92 then slowly make there way down.  . Its a very gradual but sure process.  
The other 3 seasons . Plenty to do. Hiking / over 800 miles of trails in this are of the smokies.  Endless tubing and river swimming ( rivers in the park are usually 60 to 70 degrees throughout the summer crystal clear.  Lakes all around thay are all dammed up mountain rivers.  Tubing the Little river in the Smokies has to be the our most desired.  
That reminds about the hellbender salamander.  Weve caught 2 over the last few yrs while tubing.  They are the largest of all salmanders.  Very slow about 12" in length with a massive prehistoric looking head.  
 We were up at Clingmans dome today.   The views were perfect after the storms rolled through. 62 clear blue skies to the north and the backside of the storms to the south watching the clouds roll over the mountains into the Carolinas.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: That sounds absolutely magical. Thank you for sharing that. The way you described the fireflies makes me want to experience it in person someday. What time of year do you enjoy living in the Smokies the most?

replies.author: @Rob5215life
replies.text:
I live in the Smokies. .. 
It is a very magical place.  
The fire fly phenomena starts late may and increases everyday till the its culmination around first week of July. 
 The fireflies take flight to the tree tops around 10 pm. It looks like twinkling christmas lights. 
 The things to do and see here are endless.

replies.author: @TruePlanetOffical
replies.text: Thanks so much! I'm glad those details stood out to you—they amazed me too. Have you ever hiked any part of the Appalachian Trail? And if you have any ideas for making future videos better, I'd love to hear them!
